sentences.text: She was miserable.
She was miserable.
She didn't have nothing.
She had a little water.
And she had soup.
And she had bread.
Cannot tell that she didn't have bread and soup.
Nobody was hungry.
If somebody will tell you that was hungry, that's not true.
They tried to everybody to give bread and soup.
Oh, when a girl had already somebody, and he went for [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what is a [NON-ENGLISH]?
He went out with the other partisans to collect food.
So he putting always something for her.
He was putting a piece of butter, a piece of meat, whatever he could find, what the partisans didn't see.
But everybody was doing this.
So she had something more, and she was happy.
He will bring her a pair of pants.
He will bring her a pair of shoes.
She was warm.
And she was sheltered.
And she knew that she's not by herself.
So the position with the men was better than by herself.
Not too many were by themselves.
Mostly all of them, they find somebody.
I will stay till today with them, after the war.
I don't think many divorced, no, not many.
But there are a couple, a couple that divorced.
It didn't work.
They run away from them.
Because it was too ugly thing.
But mostly, they're staying, staying for 50 years.
[INAUDIBLE] They were calling them [NON-ENGLISH].
If a girl find a man, and she was to sleep with him, she was together with him in the zimlanka.
Zimlanka is a place in the wintertime.
They couldn't sleep upstairs, under the tree, because of the snow.
And they were cold.
So they built under the tree, they built a zimlanka, and they were there.
They're putting fire, fire it was.
And they sleep there.
And they ate there.
And they cooked there, only at night.
Because in the forest, even when you cooking at night, you can see the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH].
The-- Smoke.
Yes, the smoke.
So they were cooking very, very late.
But everybody have what to eat.
Everybody was.
Per zimlanka?
About 20, 25 people.
No, mostly they were choosing with whom to stay.
I never had a zimlanka.
I was staying always with my husband in a village, in another village everyday in another place.
In other place, because we were staying always between the German, with Poland, and Ukrainian inside.
We were surrounded.
They were surrounding us.
How come?
Because he was in the-- he had to see-- wait a second.
He was the commander of the whole partisans of the information, where is everybody, how many Ukrainian, how many Cossacks, how many Germans are here.
He had to be always on counting on them.
You understand?
He couldn't lose his head on that.
So he was always up.
Sometimes Tuvia was in the puszta.
A puszta is a place where the wild-- wild, wild arbors and [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] animals.
There you could sleep.
And you can do everything what you wanted.
Nobody could come to them.
If somebody would come, they knew right away.
Because this is from the Puszta Naliboki was from the cities, about 50 miles, 50 kilometer.
That's far.
You understand?
Puszta Naliboki was--
Puszta Naliboki is with Novogrudok, Minsk, and Lida.
The forest where the [NON-ENGLISH] group was.
Naliboki, how you spell it?
N-A-L-I-B-O-K-I.
Naliboiki.
This was the biggest puszta on the border from Poland and Russia.
Yeah.
Yes.
So there, they lived already.
They had their bread, and they had bakeries, and they had people was working on shuls.
People were doing everything what you needed for the people.
There were verkstad.
Workshops was in the Puszta Naliboki, yes.
But when the German was surrounding on before the puszta we didn't have it whom to fight.
Because everybody, Zusia was the fighter, and Asael.
And he began to fight.
He didn't see nobody with him.
Everybody was running away, to take his girlfriends, and his wife, to go in some place.
He didn't want to fight.
So he knew that something has to be done.
They will not survive.
So then they decided, and the Russian, with the Russian, they had to take it out the young from the Bielskis.
Was [NON-ENGLISH].
This was-- [NON-ENGLISH] was from the Bielskis, the very young men.
They were the fighters.
They didn't stay in the puszta.
They stayed with Zusia on the [NON-ENGLISH], between the German, Polish, and Ukrainians.
And if Zusia would not stay with them, they would never have bread in the puszta.
Because there was so many partisan Polish they were fighting for a piece of bread.
They were taking away from the [?
Belshikes ?].
They call them, the partisans Bielskis, [?
Bershikes ?].
They were taking away the horses.
They're taking away the meat.
They're taking away everything from them.
The Russian partisan.
They stopped, my husband stopped them once.
I went with him, and about 15 riders.
I was with him then.
And I saw the vans of the Bielskis.
And nobody was there.
The vans, the vans.
And the horses from the Bielskis, because my husband knew them.
And he saw a couple of boys sitting and waiting.
He said, what are you doing here?
What are you waiting?
What is it?
He said, you know?
They took away all the food, and took away all the food.
And they took away all the [NON-ENGLISH], no?
The ammunition.
And what now?
So he says, what now?
You give away the ammunition, and the bread, and everything?
You are partisans?
You too partisans.
What the matter with you?
You give away your life?
To whom did you give?
He said, they say, they're all in the house.
And this was Zusia's very good friends.
The partisan, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a Russian partisan.
Wait, I will tell you what's his name.
Panchenko.
Panchenko.
And they were sitting.
They're drinking.
And they had all the ammunition from them.
And nothing.
Zusia came in.
I didn't go.
I was with the others outside.
And he said to him, what did you do with my partisans?
He said, that's not your partisans.
That's from the Naliboki.
He said, mine too.
What did you?
We took away.
Because they robbed too much the paisanos.
What?
You took away from my partisans?
You took away the ammunition and everything?
Who gave it to you the right?
So Panchenko took right away the [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what is [NON-ENGLISH]?
This is something what they make about 60 rounds, with a round.
Is that the machine gun?
A machine gun.
And he point there in a machine gun to Zusia.
So we had-- in [NON-ENGLISH] we had a Russian commander.
I forgot his name.
OK?
And he clubbed the-- and this stopped.
He said, listen, Panchenko.
You better give to Alex everything if you know what's good for you.
And in two minutes, they drank together.
And he gave away everything.
And he said, Zusia to him, remember we were two years very good friends.
But if you will be like that, I will not give you my friendship.
But I will kill you.
I remember one thing.
This is the first and the last time you should do something like that.
And they became very good friends.
They give away all the food.
They give away everything.
And Zusia send them back to the puszta.
If Zusia would not be there, they would not have the food and nothing.
Zusia was always under a [INAUDIBLE].
If somebody went out from Tuvia's people there, he was always watching on them.
Yes.
Yeah, and he was watching always on them.
And this is the time we need to watch them.
Because there were Polacks there.
And they were worse than the Germans.
This was the white Polacks.
And they were terrible.
They will find a Jewish girl, or Jewish boy, they were to kill them.
Take away the boots.
And nobody knew what happened to them.
Till we caught them.
And put about five-- killed them.
And no more was something like that.
You see, you have to be very smart with them.
You killing.
I will show you.
I know how to kill too.
And this was the only medicine for them.
That's it.
That was a very good medicine.
The Russian?
The Russian are very good people.
The Russian, who they came from Moscow, where they send them, they were very nice.
And they said like that.
They would come.
The Bielskis always went to Platon.
He was the head, from the whole the partisans.
And they were complaining that the Russian and the partisans, they hated Jews.
And they taking away this and this.
He said, [INAUDIBLE], you know what?
As long as we are existing, as long as we have Russia, Poland, and German, they hate a Jew.
But after the war, you get your say too.
You will get your say.
You can do nothing now to them.
Now if you have more guts, kill them.
If he's doing something to you, kill them.
That's all.
In the beginning only.
In the beginning in '42.
After this, never was together.
Never was together, because they couldn't.
If they went in some place, even they took the things for the German, they were always telling that this belongs to me.
And this belongs to me.
And this belongs to them.
And they didn't want to fight.
You understand?
They didn't.
The Bielskis didn't want to fight the Russian, you understand?
There were too many.
So they decided that no more.
We will do whatever we have to do by ourselves.
And you do whatever you can by yourselves.
What about fighting Germans?
From Moscow they were sending that to Zusia that he has to take a couple of Cossacks.
And to send to Moscow, they should know what are the Cossack doing, whether preparing for the German, you understand?
So this was about a whole week he was looking for them.
And he find them.
He find them.
And he send them away.
They killed four or five till they took them.
But they took them.
Were the Bielskis partisans ever in any sabotage missions?
I cannot call this sabotage.
We heard about it, that they want to cancel a ghetto, [INAUDIBLE].
That's not far from Novogrudok.
With Novogrudok, and [INAUDIBLE], and Lida.
The ghettos, small ghetto.
So the Bielskis brother decided that we have to go, and to take out the Jew from the ghetto.
Yeah?
So this was a very good group.
Tuvia went, and Asael, and Zusia, and Ivan, with Sonia-- Tuvia's Sonia, and Chaya.
Because I didn't speak to Sonia, Tuvia's.
There were two houses.
One house was Sonia, with the [NON-ENGLISH].
And the other house was me and Chaya with another [NON-ENGLISH].
But to have 10 people--
[NON-ENGLISH] is the-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] to-to-to-to-to!
The--
Yes.
Machine guns, very good ones.
So they didn't let us by themselves.
They left us with five or 10 partisans with very good machine guns, one house for Sonia.
And the other house I was with Chaya.
And they went away to take the Jew out from the ghetto.
Somebody told them that the Bielskis are here.
Absolute, this is, they knew, that somebody, maybe from the partisan from the Russian partisan, or from the Polish partisan.
They said to the Germans, that the Bielskis are stationed here.
And see in [NON-ENGLISH].
And they came.
They took us around the two houses.
First of all, first house was Sonia's, Sonia's Tuvia's.
And they were fighting terrible.
Till all of them, they got killed.
With Sonia.
The partisan now were killed, all of them.
The 10, and Sonia was between them.
And this was the sister.
Lilka was not there.
No.
Sonia Bielski, me.
This was Sonia, Tuvia's wife.
Yeah, was a Bielski, OK.
She got killed.
When they were fighting in the first house, [INAUDIBLE] went out from the house, from the back, from the second house.
And we run away.
They began to shoot on us.
But they couldn't find us.
Because this was many, many trees.
And the snow was still here.
And I was asleep without shoes, with nothing.
And Chaya said to me, Sonia, German are around.
You have to stand up.
Go.
I said, I cannot go.
I don't have nothing.
You're going.
And I went without nothing.
And it was very cold.
And I was feeling till here, this snow.
And I couldn't go.
When I am very afraid of something, I'm losing myself.
I lost everything.
And I said, Chaya, you go my kin, and you tell how do I fell.
She said, oh no.
I don't want fell from Zusia's hand.
Oh, no.
I'm not going without you.
And they were a one [INAUDIBLE].
This was a machine gun.
And he said, Chaya, if you're not going, I'm going away.
I will not wait for Sonia.
He said, you can go.
I'm not leaving Sonia.
And she took me for the hand.
And she took me out from there.
And that's why I'm alive today, because of her.
And so it took us about two nights, two or three nights.
He went into a house.
And he took something for me to wear.
And we came to the Bielskis.
We knew right away, we knew always if something happen, we will know where to find them.
You understand?
When they came in, all the houses was already in fire.
Tuvia came and Zusia and Asael.
They saw that the whole is in a fire.
They were looking.
The German went away already.
Because everything was killed.
So they were looking.
They saw something.
They saw the boys and the girls.
But they couldn't find me.
And they couldn't find Chaya.
The other was too very-- they were thinking that they see something.
But they were burned to nothing.
And they came to the places where the partisans were staying.
So they knew that maybe somebody went out from second house.
But they didn't know that me and Chaya and [INAUDIBLE].
And after three, two nights, we came.
And when they saw us, this was something like [NON-ENGLISH].
They couldn't believe it.
And then this was [NON-ENGLISH].
Chaya gave it to me the life today.
Chaya.
Very, little.
Very little, because they couldn't find a language together.
OK.
Our [NON-ENGLISH] was very big when they already divided.
So the [NON-ENGLISH]-- the people, the wives, and the children, what they were there.
And all they were already in the [NON-ENGLISH].
Right?
Only Zusia with the fighters was in the [NON-ENGLISH], in between.
So this was not enough for the Russian, this partisan.
They were in the hundreds.
And Zusia had 150, everything on the horses.
And he had another thing.
He had to report to Platon.
Where are the German?
Where are the Ukraine, where everything?
This is something else.
If they find somebody on the road, they were killing them right.
And Zusia knew the forest like he knew the five fingers, all of them.
Not only Zusia, Asael knew.
I don't know if Tuvia knew.
But all of them.
Aharon knew the forest better than somebody else.
Yes.
Zusia, yes.
If the German are here, or there, or where are they.
That was his-- this.
He was the commander of the information.
No.
No, no no.
Only to Platon.
Platon was sent from Moscow.
And we have to report to Platon and nothing, nobody else.
No.
I will tell you why.
The Russian partisans, they were from the army for the run away from the army.
When the German came, they were running.
The whole army went away, the Russian.
Everybody went to another place.
They were killing them like dogs.
So wherever they could find a place, he went to this.
After when the German went further, going to Moscow, they went out.
But they were already soldiers.
They know how to fight.
We didn't fight like soldiers.
We couldn't fight like soldiers.
We didn't have the education.
We were fighting from behind.
We were fighting behind the trees.
You understand?
We couldn't fight the army, the German.
They could fight.
They could fight.
They were fighting.
But we could not.
We were staying in a place till here in puszta, in water and in [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH].
What is [NON-ENGLISH]?
Mud?
Mud!
We were staying till here, a whole week.
And then we heard the German talking.
And they were shooting over us.
They were so near us, over us, but they couldn't go into the mud.
That you weren't got out, to the mud.
Even didn't believe that we can stay in the mud.
We stayed because all day to here to mud, hungry!
When they went away, the German, we went out from the mud.
We tried to clean up.
And then Zusia by himself said, whoever wants to go out from here, come with me.
I'm not staying.
He couldn't take the hunger.
I'm not staying here.
I'm going out from the puszta.
And who wants to go with me, let's go.
And he took about 100 to 90 people, young.
And he took me.
And we went after the German.
And this was the beginning that we went out from the puszta.
After the German went to the front.
They took off, but our yearn is to fight the partisans in the puszta,
We went over the [NON-ENGLISH], from the puszta to our places in the forest near Novogrudok, Lida, and like that.
And they went out 10 people 15 people after us.
And little by little, they left [INAUDIBLE].
How did I what?
Oh.
In the beginning, we were knocking at the door.
And they took everything from them.
But later, we couldn't take from them everything.
Because they needed to eat too.
And they had to pay so much, so much, in food to the German.
To the villagers, the villagers.
So they said to the villagers, when are you giving the food to the Germans?
So they said, the food has to be delivered tomorrow or a day after tomorrow.
OK.
So you delivering the food to the German, and we will take this away.
This was OK.
And that what it is.
But at the beginning, they were taking away the food, everything from them.
But later couldn't take away, because Platon said, that's impossible to take away the last food from them.
Because they will kill us by themselves, the villagers.
You understand?
So we tried to not to take them.
Although we would take them, took from the food what they were taking, preparing for the German.
We took away.
We had enough food for everything.
We had meat, we had bread.
We had everything.
How was the food divided among the people?
This was never even divided.
The command chef, they had [INAUDIBLE] and dinners, the best from everything.
The rest, they had soup kitchens.
They had bread.
And they had meat.
So they divided the meat too.
Now they had factories for salamis, Pesach [INAUDIBLE] He was by the food.
So he was dividing the food.
Somebody came.
And said, he want the salamis.
So they divide a whole salami.
Friedberg.
He was dividing the food.
But they had.
It was not hungry.
Nobody was hungry.
No.
Per zimlanka.
Per zimlanka.
Yes.
Separate.
Beginning it was communal.
But then they begin to cook by themselves.
Everybody, like the cooking, other ones.
The young didn't have the job.
Because the young would always, for food, the young, the capable.
The older people, they were working the jobs.
They were making salamis.
They were baking bread.
They were making shoes.
They were making pants.
Even the Russian partisan, they were coming to Tuvia, there to the [INAUDIBLE].
And they were fixing everything for them.
The pants, the shoes.
This was called, [NON-ENGLISH].
They came.
They were calling [NON-ENGLISH].
Yeah.
And they were fixing the thing.
Right.
If they want something, they were coming.
They said OK.
I wanted a salami, a good one.
They were making salamis there.
We had people that came from the ghettos.
And they knew how to make salamis, and made salamis.
They were not kosher, but they were making.
My mother, three and a half years didn't have a piece of meat.
They were working the kitchen.
They were working in the [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH].
You know what is [NON-ENGLISH]?
[NON-ENGLISH] is where the whole partisan, they were working there.
Administration, right.
Not too many work.
Because when they went already to the puszta.
Everything was already done.
They knew who is in the administration.
And they knew how to work.
Everybody was working for themselves in their place, in the zimlanka.
They were cooking.
